beats.narration_text: Merhaba — this is Kore, in for Humanitarians AI. You are making a chart. The graphing skill gives you chartkit primitives — theme, palette, finish, save, and write_html — and a four-step workflow. The philosophy: you make the decisions about chart type, colors, and story. The kit handles the consistent parts. Let me walk you through the primitives and the gaps.

props.command: Chart this weekly revenue data as a bar chart with the company brand colors.
output: chartkit: theme() · palette() · finish() · save() · write_html()
Four steps: look at data → infer colors → write script → render and look
Judgement, not flags — chart type from data shape, not defaults

beats.narration_text: The kit has five primitives and three data helpers. theme sets matplotlib rcParams and returns resolved colors. The key field is that foreground colors derive from background luminance — pass a dark background and you get a correct dark chart automatically. palette builds n colors from a base hex, cycles a list, or falls back to the series defaults. finish adds the typographic frame: left-aligned bold title, muted subtitle, and provenance caption. save writes PNG, SVG, or both and returns paths. write_html produces a self-contained interactive page by inlining React, ReactDOM, react-is, and Recharts from a local third-party directory — the file opens offline with no CDN dependency. The three data helpers address specific gotchas. zero_fill_days fills calendar gaps in a timeseries — but skip it when zeros would be a lie because the data is sparsely sampled, not absent. rolling_mean is trailing and early points average whatever exists so far — do not center it. log_floor gives a lower bound for bar charts on log scales where value equals the axis minimum. All three are optional — use them only when they fit. The absolute path to graphing's scripts directory must go into sys.path — not a relative path, because the working directory is the user's project.

props.sparkLine: theme() derives colors from luminance. write_html inlines offline. zero_fill_days: skip if zeros would lie.

beats.narration_text: The four steps are: look at the data and decide what it deserves; infer colors from context — tailwind config, CSS variables, brand guidelines, and semantic meaning; write the script; and render and look at the result before handing it over. The render-and-look step is explicit: check labels for legibility, nothing overlapping, colors distinguishable, the story of the data visible. Fix and re-render until it is right. The judgement section gives defaults with explicit permission to deviate when the data argues for it. Five defaults worth knowing. Rotate x labels only when they would otherwise collide — short labels stay horizontal. Cap bar width when there are few categories. Label bars with their values when there are roughly a dozen or fewer. Rank categorical bars by value unless the categories have a natural order. Long category names read better horizontal. A title states what the chart shows — not the chart type, not the column name. Legends only when there is more than one series. Annotate the chart with what matters: a deploy line, a threshold, the peak, the anomaly. An annotation the reader did not need is clutter.

props.sparkLine: Four steps: look, infer, write, look again. Rank bars. Label small datasets. Annotate what matters.

beats.act: teardown — what it gets right / where it bites
beats.narration_text: Here is the teardown. The skill gets five things right. Look at the data and decide what it deserves — the first step forces chart-type judgment from data shape and meaning, not from a default or a flag. Color derivation from background luminance: theme auto-adapts to dark and light surfaces correctly — a dark background produces a dark chart without manual color overrides. write_html inlines all dependencies from a local third-party directory — the file opens offline with no CDN, which is unusual and correct for shareable outputs. The data helpers address real gotchas with precise remedies: zero_fill_days for gaps, rolling_mean for smoothing, log_floor for log-scale bars — and the skill says when to skip each one. The judgement section gives explicit defaults with permission to deviate — not a style guide, a starting point. Where it bites. The absolute path requirement for sys.path is documented but the examples write it as slash path slash to slash graphing slash scripts — easy to copy literally. A relative path silently fails with ModuleNotFoundError. The GRID and ACCENT strings in the interactive HTML component template are not variables — they are placeholder strings that must be manually substituted with real color values before calling write_html. If you copy the template directly, the chart has no grid and a string as its stroke. The render-and-look step says to check that labels are legible, nothing overlaps, colors are distinguishable, and the story is visible — that is four criteria in one sentence, and there is no rubric for what passes. rolling_mean edge behavior for windows smaller than w is described as average what exists so far — but what that looks like in practice for the first few points is left to inference. And the smoke test only confirms a non-empty PNG — it does not verify theme, palette, or finish individually.

props.sparkLine: Judgement-first workflow and offline write_html correct. GRID/ACCENT placeholders must be substituted manually.

beats.narration_text: Verdict. graphing: chartkit primitives — theme (luminance-derived colors), palette (n colors from base or list), finish (title + subtitle + caption frame), save (PNG or SVG), write_html (offline self-contained Recharts page). Four steps: look at data, infer colors from context, write script, render and look. Judgement defaults: chart type from shape, rank bars, label small datasets, annotate what matters, legend only for multiple series. Data helpers: zero_fill_days, rolling_mean (trailing), log_floor. Gaps: absolute sys.path required; GRID and ACCENT are literal placeholder strings in HTML template — substitute before use; no QC rubric; rolling_mean edge behavior implicit; smoke test verifies PNG only.

artifactLines: chartkit: theme() (luminance) · palette() · finish() · save() · write_html() (offline)
Four steps: look at data → infer colors → write script → render and look
Judgement defaults: chart type from shape · rank bars · label small · annotate
Data helpers: zero_fill_days · rolling_mean (trailing) · log_floor — skip when wrong
GRID and ACCENT are literal placeholder strings in interactive template — must substitute
Gaps: absolute sys.path · no QC rubric · rolling_mean edge implicit · smoke test limited

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Give Claude a CSV of weekly website visits over six months with some missing weeks and ask for a bar chart with the company brand color. Watch five things. First: does it use the absolute path to graphing's scripts directory in sys.path, or does it try a relative path? Second: does it check for missing weeks and use zero_fill_days, or does it plot the data raw with gaps on a continuous axis? Third: does it pass the brand color to palette or theme, or does it use the default series colors and not mention the brand? Fourth: does it call finish with a title that states what the chart shows, or does it use a column name? Fifth: does it read the PNG back and explicitly check the result, or does it hand over the path without looking?

props.command: Chart this CSV of weekly visits over 6 months as a bar chart in our brand blue.
props.runningText: Watch: absolute sys.path? zero_fill_days for gaps? brand color wired? finish title? render + look?
output: Expected: absolute path · zero_fill_days for missing weeks · palette(base=brand_hex)
Expected: finish(title='Weekly visits…') · read PNG back and check result
Red flag: relative path · raw gaps plotted · column name as title · no render check

beats.narration_text: graphing. Kore, in for Humanitarians AI.
